questions.text: Chlorine in Vinyl chloride is not very reactive because
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: C−Cl bond develops partial double bonds
options.explanation: In vinyl chloride, the C−Cl bond exhibits some degree of double bond character due to resonance. This is because the carbon atom involved in the C−Cl bond is sp2 hybridized and forms a pi (π) bond with the chlorine atom. The pi bond contributes to the stability of the molecule and reduces the reactivity of the C−Cl bond compared to a typical single bond.

Resonance refers to the delocalization of electrons within a molecule. While resonance does occur in vinyl chloride, it is more directly related to the partial double bond character of the C−Cl bond, as explained in option (a).

options.text: Sp2 hybridized carbon has more acidic character than sp3 hybridized carbon
options.explanation: This statement is generally not true. The acidity of a carbon atom is primarily determined by the stability of the conjugate base that forms after losing a proton. The sp2 hybridized carbon in vinyl chloride does not significantly affect its acidity in the context of the given question.

questions.text: The waves in which the particles of the medium vibrate about their mean position along the direction of propagation of the waves are called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Longitudinal waves are waves in which the particles of the medium vibrate back and forth along the direction of propagation of the wave. This means that the particles compress and expand in the same direction as the wave is traveling. Examples of longitudinal waves include sound waves in a gas or liquid, and seismic P-waves in the Earth.
In transverse waves, the particles of the medium vibrate perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave. Examples include light waves, radio waves, and seismic S-waves.

Mechanical waves is a broader category that includes both longitudinal and transverse waves, as well as surface waves and torsional waves. It refers to waves that require a physical medium to propagate.
Electromagnetic waves are waves that can propagate through a vacuum and do not require a physical medium. They include light, radio waves, X-rays, and gamma rays, and are transverse waves.

questions.text: If the waves interfere constructively, the amplitude of the resulting wave would be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Less than either of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave can never be less than either of the individual waves. This is because the crests of the two waves cannot cancel each other out completely. Even if the two waves are out of phase by 180 degrees, the troughs of the two waves will still add together to form a wave with some amplitude.

options.text: Greater than either of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave would be greater than either of the individual waves. When two waves interfere constructively, the crests of the two waves line up, resulting in a wave with an amplitude that is equal to the sum of the amplitudes of the two individual waves. For example, if two waves with amplitudes of 1 cm and 2 cm interfere constructively, the amplitude of the resulting wave will be 3 cm.

options.text: Equal to the shortest of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave can also never be equal to the shortest of the individual waves. This is because the crests of the two waves cannot be perfectly aligned if the waves have different amplitudes.

options.text: Equal to the greatest of the individual waves
options.explanation: The amplitude of the resulting wave can be equal to the greatest of the individual waves. This happens when the two waves have the same amplitude and are in phase.

options.text: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a travelling wave
options.explanation: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a travelling wave: This statement is generally incorrect. In a travelling wave, the energy of a small part of a string varies with time. As the wave propagates along the string, the kinetic and potential energies of the particles comprising the string oscillate, leading to changes in the total energy of each small part. At certain points in the wave cycle, the energy is maximum (e.g., when the displacement is maximum), and at other points, it may be minimum or zero (e.g., when the displacement is zero). Therefore, the energy of a small part of a string in a travelling wave is not constant.

options.text: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in standing wave
options.explanation: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a standing wave: This statement is generally correct. In a standing wave, the energy of a small part of a string remains relatively constant over time. This is because the oscillations in a standing wave occur at fixed positions, known as nodes and antinodes, where the amplitude of displacement is either minimum (nodes) or maximum (antinodes). At nodes, the kinetic energy is zero, and the potential energy is maximum, while at antinodes, the kinetic energy is maximum, and the potential energy is zero. Therefore, the total energy of each small part remains approximately constant as the wave oscillates.

options.text: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a travelling wave
options.explanation: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a travelling wave: This statement is generally incorrect. In a travelling wave, the energy of each small part varies depending on its position within the wave. Parts of the wave that experience maximum displacement (e.g., crest or trough) have higher energy compared to parts experiencing minimum displacement (e.g., points halfway between crest and trough). Therefore, the energies of all small parts of equal length are not equal in a travelling wave.

options.text: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a travelling wave
options.explanation: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a standing wave: This statement is generally correct. In a standing wave, the energies of all small parts of equal length are approximately equal. This is because the oscillations occur at fixed positions (nodes and antinodes), and the energy distribution along the string is relatively uniform. While there may be slight variations in energy due to damping or other factors, in an idealized standing wave, the energies of equal-length segments are nearly identical.

questions.explanation: Option A, C and D are wrong as in a travelling wave energy does not remain constant and is transferred from one point to another.However, in a standing wave as mentioned in option B, Energy of small part remains constant or STANDING in that part.

questions.text: Which one of the following wave does not transmit energy?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Mechanical waves, like sound waves and water waves, do transmit energy. When a disturbance occurs in a medium, it causes particles to oscillate, transferring energy from one point to another.

Unlike the other options, standing waves do not transmit energy. They are formed by the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions, resulting in regions of constructive and destructive interference. The energy of the wave appears to be "standing still" and does not propagate through space.
Matter waves, also known as de Broglie waves, are associated with particles, such as electrons or atoms. These waves do transmit energy and are used to describe the wave-like behavior of particles.
Electromagnetic waves, like light waves and radio waves, do transmit energy. They consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields that propagate through space.

questions.text: There is no net transfer of energy by particles of medium in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a longitudinal wave, the particles of the medium oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred through compression and rarefaction of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles pass the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy.

A progressive wave refers to a wave that propagates through a medium, carrying energy from one point to another. It can be either a longitudinal or a transverse wave. In both cases, there is a net transfer of energy as the wave moves through the medium.

In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred by the up-and-down motion of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles transmit the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy.

A stationary wave, also known as a standing wave, is formed by the superposition of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. In a stationary wave, the particles of the medium oscillate in fixed positions, resulting in regions of constructive and destructive interference. There is no net transfer of energy by the particles in a stationary wave.

questions.explanation: (a) Longitudinal wave: In a longitudinal wave, the particles of the medium oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred through compression and rarefaction of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles pass the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy. (b) Progressive wave: A progressive wave refers to a wave that propagates through a medium, carrying energy from one point to another. It can be either a longitudinal or a transverse wave. In both cases, there is a net transfer of energy as the wave moves through the medium. (c) Transverse wave: In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred by the up-and-down motion of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles transmit the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy. (d) Stationary wave: A stationary wave, also known as a standing wave, is formed by the superposition of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. In a stationary wave, the particles of the medium oscillate in fixed positions, resulting in regions of constructive and destructive interference. There is no net transfer of energy by the particles in a stationary wave. Out of these options, the correct answer is (d) stationary wave. Unlike longitudinal, progressive, and transverse waves, a stationary wave does not involve a net transfer of energy by the particles of the medium.

questions.text: Consider a stretched string under tension and fixed at both ends. If the tension is doubled and the cross-sectional area halved, then the frequency becomes:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct. The frequency is proportional to the square root of tension and inversely proportional to the square root of mass per unit length. Doubling the tension and halving the cross-sectional area increases the frequency by a factor of two.
Incorrect. This would imply the frequency decreases, which is not the case when tension is increased and mass per unit length is decreased.
Incorrect. The changes in tension and cross-sectional area do not result in a fourfold increase in frequency.
Incorrect. The frequency does not increase eightfold with the given changes in tension and cross-sectional area.

questions.explanation: In a stretched string, the frequency of the fundamental mode is given by the formula: f = (1/2L) * √(T/μ), where T is the tension, L is the length, and μ is the mass per unit length. If the tension is doubled (T becomes 2T) and the cross-sectional area is halved, then the mass per unit length (μ) is also halved. Since frequency f is proportional to the square root of T/μ, doubling T and halving μ results in the frequency being multiplied by √(2/0.5) = √(4) = 2. Therefore, the frequency becomes twice its original value. The options 'Half', 'Four times', and 'Eight times' are incorrect as they do not correctly reflect the mathematical relationship between tension, mass per unit length, and frequency.

SO32– is produced in the second dissociation step of H2SO3, which is less favorable than the first, leading to its very low concentration. Thus, this option is correct.
H+ ions are produced in both dissociation steps and from water itself, leading to a higher concentration than SO32–. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Water is the solvent and is present in large excess compared to any of the dissociated species, so its concentration is not the lowest. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In a solution of sulfurous acid, H2SO3, which is a weak diprotic acid, dissociation occurs in two steps. The first dissociation step forms H+ and HSO3–, while the second, less favorable step forms H+ and SO32–. Since the second dissociation step is less complete than the first, the concentration of SO32– is the lowest among the species in solution. The concentrations of H2SO3, HSO3–, and H+ are higher because they are involved in the more favorable first dissociation step or are present due to the dissociation of water. Water itself is present in vast excess, so its concentration is not the least. Therefore, SO32– is present in the lowest concentration.

questions.text: Which of the following can conduct electricity in a solid state?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: FeCl3 (s) is an ionic compound. Ionic compounds typically conduct electricity when dissolved in water or in a molten state because the ions are free to move, but they do not conduct in solid form. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Iodine, I2 (s) is a molecular solid formed by non-metal atoms. Non-metals generally do not conduct electricity because they lack free electrons or ions. Hence, this option is incorrect.

NaOH(s) is an ionic compound. Like most ionic solids, it only conducts electricity when dissolved in water or melted, as the ions are then free to move. In a solid state, it does not conduct electricity. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Sucrose is a covalent compound composed of non-metal atoms. Covalent compounds generally do not conduct electricity because they do not have free-moving charged particles. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Graphite is a form of carbon where each carbon atom is bonded to three others, creating layers of hexagonal rings. The fourth electron of each carbon atom is delocalized, allowing it to move freely and carry an electric current between the layers, making graphite an excellent conductor of electricity in its solid state.

questions.explanation: Graphite is the only substance listed that can conduct electricity in the solid state. This is due to its unique structure of carbon atoms bonded in layers with delocalized electrons that can move freely between the layers, allowing for the conduction of electricity. The other options, such as FeCl3, NaOH, and I2, are either ionic or covalent compounds that lack free-moving charged particles in their solid states, thus not conducting electricity. Sucrose, being a covalent compound made of non-metals, similarly lacks this capacity for conduction.

questions.text: Mark out the correct option.
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a travelling wave
options.explanation: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a travelling wave: This statement is generally incorrect. In a travelling wave, the energy of a small part of a string varies with time. As the wave propagates along the string, the kinetic and potential energies of the particles comprising the string oscillate, leading to changes in the total energy of each small part. At certain points in the wave cycle, the energy is maximum (e.g., when the displacement is maximum), and at other points, it may be minimum or zero (e.g., when the displacement is zero). Therefore, the energy of a small part of a string in a travelling wave is not constant.

options.text: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in standing wave
options.explanation: The energy of any small part of a string remains constant in a standing wave: This statement is generally correct. In a standing wave, the energy of a small part of a string remains relatively constant over time. This is because the oscillations in a standing wave occur at fixed positions, known as nodes and antinodes, where the amplitude of displacement is either minimum (nodes) or maximum (antinodes). At nodes, the kinetic energy is zero, and the potential energy is maximum, while at antinodes, the kinetic energy is maximum, and the potential energy is zero. Therefore, the total energy of each small part remains approximately constant as the wave oscillates.

options.text: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a travelling wave
options.explanation: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a travelling wave: This statement is generally incorrect. In a travelling wave, the energy of each small part varies depending on its position within the wave. Parts of the wave that experience maximum displacement (e.g., crest or trough) have higher energy compared to parts experiencing minimum displacement (e.g., points halfway between crest and trough). Therefore, the energies of all small parts of equal length are not equal in a travelling wave.

options.text: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a travelling wave
options.explanation: The energies of all small parts of equal length are equal in a standing wave: This statement is generally correct. In a standing wave, the energies of all small parts of equal length are approximately equal. This is because the oscillations occur at fixed positions (nodes and antinodes), and the energy distribution along the string is relatively uniform. While there may be slight variations in energy due to damping or other factors, in an idealized standing wave, the energies of equal-length segments are nearly identical.

questions.explanation: Option A, C and D are wrong as in a travelling wave energy does not remain constant and is transferred from one point to another.However, in a standing wave as mentioned in option B, Energy of small part remains constant or STANDING in that part.

questions.text: Which of the following oxides is the most basic?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: SiO2 is an acidic oxide, common for nonmetals like silicon. It does not exhibit basic properties, making this option incorrect.
Al2O3 is an amphoteric oxide, which means it can react both as an acid and a base, but it is not predominantly basic. Hence, this option is incorrect.
MgO is a basic oxide, typical for Group IIA elements, but it is less basic compared to Group IA oxides due to the lower electropositivity of magnesium. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
Na2O is a highly basic oxide due to the high electropositivity of sodium, a Group IA element, which forms strong bases. This makes it the correct choice.

questions.explanation: Na2O is the most basic oxide among the options provided because it is formed by a Group IA element, sodium, known for its strong basic properties due to its high electropositivity. In contrast, SiO2 is acidic, Al2O3 is amphoteric, and MgO, although basic, is less so than Na2O because magnesium is less electropositive than sodium.

questions.text: When solid aluminum chloride reacts with a few drops of water, it produces a steamy cloud of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Chlorine gas (Cl2) is not formed in the reaction between aluminum chloride and water. The reaction produces hydrogen chloride (HCl) instead. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
Hydrogen gas (H2) is not produced when aluminum chloride reacts with water. The correct gaseous product is hydrogen chloride (HCl). Thus, this option is incorrect.
When solid aluminum chloride reacts with water, it releases hydrogen chloride (HCl) gas, which forms a steamy cloud. This is the correct answer.AlCl3 + 3H2O → Al(OH)3 + 3HCl
Hypochlorous acid (HOCl) is not a product of the reaction between aluminum chloride and water. The actual products are aluminum hydroxide and hydrogen chloride. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The reaction between solid aluminum chloride (AlCl3) and water results in the formation of aluminum hydroxide (Al(OH)3) and hydrogen chloride gas (HCl). The release of HCl gas forms a steamy cloud. The balanced chemical equation is:AlCl3 + 3H2O → Al(OH)3 + 3HCl Option C is correct as it identifies HCl as the gaseous product. Options A, B, and D are incorrect as Cl2, H2, and HOCl are not formed in this reaction.

questions.text: Which of the following options is CORRECT for [Ni(NH2CH2CH2NH2)3]2+
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Coordination Number: 6 Type of Ligand: Bidentate
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The coordination number refers to the total number of coordinate bonds formed between the central metal and the ligands. Each NH2CH2CH2NH2 is a bidentate ligand, meaning it can donate two pairs of electrons to the metal center. With three such ligands, the total coordination number is 3 x 2 = 6.

options.text: Coordination Number: 6 Type of Ligand: Hexadentate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Though the coordination number is correctly identified as 6, NH2CH2CH2NH2 is not a hexadentate ligand. It is a bidentate ligand as it has only two donor atoms.

options.text: Coordination Number: 3 Type of Ligand: Bidentate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While NH2CH2CH2NH2 is indeed a bidentate ligand, the coordination number should be 6, not 3. Each ligand forms two bonds with the metal, resulting in a coordination number of 3 x 2 = 6.

This option is incorrect. NH2CH2CH2NH2 is a bidentate ligand, not hexadentate, and the coordination number should be 6, not 3. Each ligand forms two bonds, leading to a coordination number of 3 x 2 = 6.

questions.explanation: In the complex [Ni(NH2CH2CH2NH2)3]2+, the nickel center is surrounded by three NH2CH2CH2NH2 ligands. Each of these ligands is bidentate, meaning it can form two coordinate covalent bonds with the nickel ion. Therefore, the total coordination number for this complex is 6 (3 ligands x 2 bonds each). Option A correctly identifies both the coordination number and the type of ligand. Options B, C, and D are incorrect either because they misidentify the coordination number or the nature of the ligand.
